results.possibleIdentity: Richard M. Jouharian (1946–2003) was an American abstract artist based in St. Louis, Missouri, who was active during the late 1960s and early 1970s. His artistic practice was characterized by the use of modern synthetic and industrial materials, often creating paintings with acrylic on plastic, vinyl, or mylar mounted on canvas. His work is associated with the experimental art movements of the era that explored the intersection of art and technology, a focus shared by the organization Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.). Jouharian's art has been held in the collection of the Mildred Lane Kemper Art Museum at Washington University in St. Louis. Notable works from his career include '70-25' (circa 1970) and various large-scale untitled compositions dated to 1969 and 1970. His estate and works have appeared in auctions through houses such as Wright and Bonhams, frequently showcasing his experimentation with non-traditional surfaces like vinyl and plastic over canvas.

results.possibleIdentity: Walter Bilger is a California-based artist and counselor who has been active in the art world since at least the early 1970s. He is currently known for his work in fused glass art, which he showcases through his practice, ClearJoy. He holds a Master of Arts in Fine Art from Humboldt State University and a Master of Arts in Social Science from the Phillips Graduate Institute. Bilger has been part of the Monterey Peninsula art community for several decades, and his professional experience spans over 30 years. In March 1973, he registered a copyright for a work of art titled "Cloudlike" (GU51247), confirming his activity as an artist during that period. In addition to his artistic career, he has worked as a counselor and maintains a spiritual or therapeutic dimension to his creative practice. He resides in Seaside, California.

Walter Gibson "Walt" Bilger (1940–2016) was an accomplished watercolorist and advertising professional. A 1963 graduate of Pennsylvania State University with a degree in Business Administration, he was a member of the Theta Chi fraternity. After serving as a Captain in the Army, he entered the field of advertising, which took him to Phoenix, Arizona, in 1974 where he worked as an account executive for Jennings & Thompson. Throughout his life, he was a member of the Harrisburg Art Association and was noted for gifting his paintings to people across five continents. He lived in Phoenix until 2013 and passed away in Vancouver, Washington.

results.possibleIdentity: Charles Leitner is a contemporary artist and poet, known for his work in the 'Poem Brut' series and visual art. In 2020, during the COVID-19 lockdown in London, he produced a series of large-scale paintings and prints, often created on scavenged cardboard. His work, such as 'So Fondly Do They Sing,' explores themes of displacement, medical trauma (specifically oncological traumas like non-Hodgkin's lymphoma), and lived realities. While he is an active artist in the 21st century, there is no direct evidence in the search results linking him specifically to the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization during the late 1960s or 1970s.

Charles Leitner (also known as Chuck Leitner) is a prominent real estate executive who has served as the Global CEO of CBRE Global Investors and the CEO of Berkshire Group. A veteran of the commercial real estate industry with over four decades of experience, he has held leadership roles at RREEF Property Trust and the Urban Land Institute (ULI) Greenprint Center for Building Performance. While he has been an active professional since the late 1970s or early 1980s and is associated with sustainable development and institutional investment, there is no verified record of him participating as an artist or collaborator in the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) group.

The description provided—an artist active in the late 1960s and 1970s associated with Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.)—matches the Austrian artist and architect Bernhard Leitner (born 1938). Bernhard Leitner moved to New York in 1968 and began pioneering experiments in 'sound-space' and 'acoustic-haptic' architecture. He is famous for his 'Soundcube' (1969), a three-dimensional matrix of loudspeakers designed to create architectural spaces using sound. His work was frequently featured in E.A.T. related contexts and publications, and he remains a leading figure in the intersection of art, architecture, and technology. It is possible the name 'Charles' is a misrecollection of 'Bernhard' in this context.

results.possibleIdentity: James Connelly (sometimes spelled James Connolly) is a contemporary Chicago-based video, sound, and new media artist, educator, and archivist. He is an Associate Professor, Adjunct at the School of the Art Institute of Chicago (SAIC) and serves as the Collection Manager of the Roger Brown Study Collection. His artistic practice involves hacking and repurposing analog and digital systems, specifically using modified cathode ray tube (CRT) monitors and custom-built video synthesizers to examine hidden power structures and aesthetic materialities. He is known for his project "Cracked Ray Tube," a collaboration with Kyle Evans, and for developing the RGB.VGA.VOLT video synthesis system first released in 2014. While his personal artistic career is primarily contemporary (with significant activity noted from the late 2000s onwards), his work is deeply rooted in the historical practices of 1970s video synthesis, such as the Sandine Image Processor developed by Dan Sandeen, and he often engages with the archives and legacies of artists active in the 1960s and 1970s, such as Roger Brown and Phil Morton.

James Connelly was a technical professional and engineer at Bell Labs in the early 1960s, a period coinciding with the formation of Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.). In 1961, he was identified as a "young Bell engineer" based in the Red Bank/Holmdel, New Jersey area, where he served as a co-chairman for local planning and engineering initiatives. Given that E.A.T. was founded in 1966 by engineers from Bell Labs (most notably Billy Klüver and Fred Waldhauer) to facilitate collaborations between artists and scientists, it is possible that Connelly was one of the technical collaborators or engineers who provided support for artists' projects during the late 1960s and early 1970s. During this era, engineers often played critical, though sometimes less publicized, roles in the execution of complex technological artworks, such as those featured in the '9 Evenings: Theatre and Engineering' (1966) or the Pepsi Pavilion at Expo '70.

results.possibleIdentity: Helmut Amann is a visual artist and filmmaker who was active in the late 1960s and 1970s. He is most notably recognized as the director of the 1970 film adaptation of Oskar Schlemmer's "Das triadische Ballett" (The Triadic Ballet), a landmark Bauhaus work. This film project was a major interdisciplinary collaboration produced by Bavaria Atelier for Südfunk Stuttgart (SDR/SWR), involving the reconstruction of Schlemmer's avant-garde kinetic and mechanical stage designs for a modern media format. Amann's work in this period fits the profile of an artist associated with the "Experiments in Art and Technology" (E.A.T.) movement, which focused on the intersection of art, media, and technological innovation. In 2016, his film was featured in the Whitney Museum of American Art exhibition "Dreamlands: Immersive Cinema and Art, 1905–2016," highlighting its significance in the history of experimental and immersive artistic technology.

Helmut Amann is a New York-based contemporary artist who began his professional career in the early 1970s. Born in Stuttgart, Germany, he immigrated to North America and eventually attended the Pratt Institute, where he received his BFA in 1970. His practice has historically focused on abstract painting, shaped canvases, and three-dimensional wall constructions, often utilizing technical materials such as metallic powders, iridescent pigments, and Mylar. Amann has also engaged with digital imagery and technology throughout his career, aligning with the interdisciplinary goals of organizations like Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.). He exhibited at the Razor Gallery and OK Harris in the 1970s and has continued to work in New York and Kingston, NY.

Hermann Amann (born 1934) is a French visual artist of German origin, active in Paris from the 1960s onward. He is recognized for his focus on the energy of color and founded the movement "Nouvelle Pigmentation" in 1986. In the early 1970s (around 1971), he founded the artistic group "Jacob ou la Persuasion" with several collaborators, including Jean-Claude Marquette. Marquette was a significant figure in the early history of computer art and a member of Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.). Hermann Amann's involvement with Marquette and other early tech-influenced artists in Paris places him within the circle of European artists associated with the E.A.T. network during that era.
